answer:
Yes—there is a fairly solid research base on this, though it usually talks about **intrinsic job characteristics** rather than “interestingness” per se.

## Short version

Across organizational psychology, the most consistent finding is:

- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich work** is a strong predictor of **job satisfaction** and often of **overall well-being**.
- **Higher pay** also helps, but its relationship to happiness/job satisfaction is usually **smaller** once pay is already good/fair.
- **Fit** matters a lot: if you deeply value leading people, then a management opportunity may increase well-being even if the subject matter is less interesting.
- There is **much less direct research on “regret”** from this exact tradeoff, but the literature on **intrinsic vs extrinsic goals** generally suggests that choices aligned with intrinsic interests/values age better psychologically than choices made mainly for money/status.

## What the research says

### 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
In the research literature, this shows up under things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **learning / challenge**
- **meaningful work**

A major meta-analysis on **work design** found that these kinds of job characteristics have **moderate to strong associations** with job satisfaction, motivation, engagement, and lower burnout/turnover.

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a very large body of work on job design.
- Their general result: jobs with more autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback tend to produce **substantially better attitudes and well-being** than jobs lacking those features.

So on the narrow question “do people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier at work?” the answer is basically **yes, on average**.

---

### 2) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect for day-to-day satisfaction
There is also a meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**. The result is not “money doesn’t matter”; it does. But the link is usually **positive and fairly small**, especially compared with richer features of the job itself.

- **Judge et al. (2010)**: pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the effect is **modest**.
- Roughly speaking, the association between pay and job satisfaction is usually **smaller** than the association between job design / intrinsic features and job satisfaction.

This fits a broader well-being literature:

- More income tends to improve **life evaluation**, security, and options.
- But people also **adapt** to income.
- Meanwhile, the nature of the work affects your **daily experience** over and over again.

For someone already at a high compensation level, the marginal happiness return from “a bit more salary” is often smaller than the return from changing the actual quality of daily work—though of course individual cases differ.

---

### 3) Intrinsic goals tend to beat extrinsic goals for well-being
A big theme in motivation research is that **intrinsic aspirations** (growth, mastery, meaningful contribution, relationships, self-development) are associated with better well-being than **extrinsic aspirations** (money, image, status), especially once basic needs are met.

Key lines of work here:

- **Self-Determination Theory**: Deci & Ryan; Gagné & Deci (workplace review)
- **Kasser & Ryan**: people who prioritize extrinsic goals more strongly tend to show worse well-being outcomes than those prioritizing intrinsic goals

That does **not** mean salary/status are irrelevant. It means that if the decision is mainly “interesting work vs somewhat more money,” the literature generally tilts toward **intrinsic quality of the work** as the more durable source of satisfaction.

---

### 4) Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction
There are meta-analyses showing that job satisfaction and life satisfaction are **moderately correlated** and likely influence each other over time.

So this is not just about “work happiness.” If one option makes your work days noticeably better, that often affects broader well-being too.

- The relationship is not perfect, but it is strong enough that work quality is not a trivial variable in overall happiness.

---

### 5) “Fit” may matter even more than any single factor
This may be the most important finding for your case.

Research on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** shows that satisfaction and retention are much higher when the role matches your values, motives, and strengths.

- **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** is a standard meta-analytic reference here.

For your situation, that means:

- If **research depth / intellectually interesting work** is core to who you are, that matters a lot.
- If **leading a team** is something you’ve genuinely wanted for a long time, that also matters a lot.
- The key is not “which option is objectively better?” but “which option better fits your deeper motives?”

Research strongly supports the idea that **value-congruent choices** tend to produce better long-term satisfaction than choices based mainly on external rewards.

---

### 6) Leadership roles are not automatically happier
Research on management/leadership roles is more mixed.

Leadership can bring:

- more autonomy
- more influence
- more meaning/impact
- more growth

But also:

- more stress
- more interruptions
- more emotional labor
- more administrative work
- less hands-on technical depth

So “I always wanted to lead a small team” is important—but the literature would say that this only improves well-being if the **day-to-day reality of leading** actually matches what you value.

In other words, “becoming a lead” is not one variable. It depends on:
- how much real people management it involves
- whether you still get enough intellectually engaging work
- whether you enjoy coaching, conflict resolution, hiring, planning, performance management, etc.

The research would not support a blanket claim that “leadership > IC” for happiness.

---

## What about regret?
This is the part where the evidence is weakest.

There is **not much direct research** that cleanly asks:

> Do people later regret choosing a more interesting job over a higher-paying but less interesting one?

But nearby literatures suggest:

1. **Intrinsic-value-congruent choices** tend to produce better long-term well-being.
2. **Extrinsic rewards** like money/status often deliver less lasting satisfaction than people forecast.
3. People often regret choices that move them away from their **ideal self** or deeply held values.

So while I wouldn’t overclaim, the research direction is roughly:

- If someone sacrifices interesting work mainly for somewhat more money/status, they are at some risk of disappointment due to adaptation.
- If someone sacrifices some compensation for work that is more engaging/meaningful and better fitted to them, that often looks better psychologically over time.
- But if leadership is itself a core intrinsic goal for you, then taking the Google role may also be an **intrinsic** move, not an extrinsic one.

That last point is important:  
**“Leading a team” is not just a perk. It may be part of the meaningful work itself.**

---

## The cleanest evidence-based takeaway

If I compress the literature into one sentence:

> Once compensation is already high enough, **the day-to-day quality of the work**—interestingness, autonomy, meaning, growth, and fit—usually matters more for sustained job satisfaction than incremental increases in pay.

And a second sentence:

> But if leadership is one of your core intrinsic aspirations, then a move into management may increase well-being despite less interesting technical content—provided you actually enjoy the lived reality of managing.

## A rough evidence ranking for long-term satisfaction
For someone in your kind of decision, the research would probably rank these as highly important:

1. **Fit with your values and motives**
2. **Interesting/meaningful day-to-day work**
3. **Autonomy / control**
4. **Growth and learning**
5. **Relationships / manager / team quality**
6. **Fair and sufficient pay**
7. **Marginal pay increases beyond that**

Not universally, but that is the broad direction of the evidence.

## Useful references to look up
If you want papers rather than summaries, these are good starting points:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design and job characteristics
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-job / person-organization fit
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — Self-Determination Theory and work motivation
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being
- **Allan et al.** — meaningful work and well-being
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and subjective well-being

If you want, I can also turn this into a **decision memo based only on the research**, e.g. a 2x2 or weighted framework specifically for:
- interesting work
- leadership opportunity
- salary delta
- future career capital
- risk of regret

Income can improve life satisfaction and security, but the impact on daily emotional well-being often shows diminishing returns as people adapt. When it comes to leadership roles, the evidence is mixed. Management might offer more control and status, but it can also bring increased stress. For first-time leaders, shifting to a role driven by status rather than a genuine desire for people management can lower well-being. As for regret, there’s limited data on long-term feelings about job interest versus pay.
**Reflecting on goals and well-being**

I'm thinking about how inaction and missed meaningful paths can affect well-being. It seems that self-concordant goals are better predictors of happiness compared to extrinsic ones. If the user wants, I could summarize 8-10 key research papers, like Hackman & Oldham or Kahnemen & Deaton, among others. It’s important to phrase things carefully, highlighting that the discussion isn't just about "interesting vs. money," but rather "research content vs. leadership aspiration." This adds a nuanced, evidence-based perspective.
**Drafting concise insights**

I'm working on drafting a summary with a clear "bottom line" and insights from the literature, maybe even including a mini table. I should mention important factors like causal caveats and selection effects. answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research on this, and the broad pattern is fairly consistent:

**People tend to be happier in work that is intrinsically interesting, meaningful, and autonomy-supporting than in work that is merely better paid.**  
But your case has an important twist: **leading a team is also an intrinsically valued goal for you**, so this is not just “interesting work vs money.” It’s closer to **interesting technical work vs a leadership path that may fit your identity and long-term goals**.

## The short version

If you compress the literature into one sentence:

> **Daily job content (interest, autonomy, meaning, fit) usually predicts job satisfaction much more strongly than pay does.**  
> **But people also do better when their work aligns with self-concordant goals** — i.e. goals they genuinely care about, such as wanting to lead.

So if the Google role is:
- **less interesting day to day**, but
- **strongly aligned with a real desire to lead and grow that way**,

then the research does **not** say “stay with the interesting work no matter what.”  
It says: **the biggest predictors of long-run satisfaction are intrinsic fit and the quality of everyday experience**, not salary alone.

---

# What the research says

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually captured through things like:
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- sometimes **challenge** and **meaning**

This comes from the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses.

### Main finding
Jobs that have these features tend to produce:
- higher **internal motivation**
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- often better **performance**

### Representative evidence
- **Hackman & Oldham (1970s)**: foundational model of motivating jobs.
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)**: meta-analysis broadly supported links between motivating job characteristics and satisfaction/motivation.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**: large meta-analysis showing work design characteristics — especially autonomy and meaningfulness-related features — are strongly related to satisfaction and other positive outcomes.

### Practical interpretation
If a job is substantially more interesting in the “I actually want to do this every day” sense, that is not a trivial perk. In the literature, it is one of the core drivers of job satisfaction.

---

## 2) Meaningful work and person-job fit matter a lot

Another robust line of research is on:
- **meaningful work**
- **person-job fit**
- **person-organization fit**
- **vocational interest fit**

### Main finding
People tend to do better when their work fits:
- their interests,
- values,
- strengths,
- and sense of identity.

This predicts:
- more satisfaction,
- more commitment,
- less burnout,
- less desire to quit.

### Representative evidence
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**: meta-analysis showing person-job/person-organization fit is meaningfully related to satisfaction and commitment.
- Research on **meaningful work** (e.g. Allan and colleagues, various meta-analytic reviews) finds fairly strong links with job satisfaction, well-being, and lower turnover intentions.
- Vocational interest-congruence research generally shows that when your work aligns with your interests, satisfaction is higher.

### Why this matters for your case
You may actually have **two different kinds of fit** in conflict:
1. **Content fit**: Meta research is very interesting to you.
2. **Role fit**: leading a team is something you’ve wanted.

Research would say both matter. The key question is not “salary or interest?” but:
> **Which role is a better overall fit for who you want to be over the next few years?**

---

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect for job satisfaction

This is one of the most replicated findings in the area.

### Main finding
Higher pay is positively related to well-being, but:
- the effect on **job satisfaction** is usually **smaller than people expect**
- the effect shows **diminishing returns**
- people often **adapt** to the higher pay

### Representative evidence
- **Judge et al.** meta-analytic work: the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is **positive but modest** — much smaller than the correlations often seen for autonomy, meaningfulness, or fit.
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**: income strongly relates to life evaluation, but emotional well-being showed weaker gains beyond a certain point.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** and later reconciliation work (**Kahneman, Killingsworth, & Mellers, 2023**) suggest the income/well-being relationship may keep rising for many people, but still: the gains are more about general life evaluation and security than about making boring work feel deeply satisfying.

### Practical interpretation
A “bit higher salary” is definitely not irrelevant. It can matter for:
- financial security
- lifestyle
- optionality
- reduced stress

But the literature does **not** suggest that a moderate salary bump reliably compensates for much worse day-to-day work quality.

---

## 4) Intrinsic motives usually outperform extrinsic motives for well-being

This comes from **Self-Determination Theory** and adjacent research.

### Main finding
People tend to thrive more when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

And when goals are **self-concordant** — meaning they reflect what the person genuinely wants, rather than what looks good externally.

### Representative evidence
- **Deci & Ryan / Self-Determination Theory**
- Research on **basic psychological need satisfaction at work** consistently links autonomy, competence, and relatedness to well-being and engagement.
- **Kasser & Ryan** and related work: pursuing primarily **extrinsic goals** (money, image, status) is generally less associated with well-being than pursuing **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery).

### Important nuance for you
If the Google offer is attractive mainly because of:
- more money,
- prestige,
- “Google looks great,”

then the literature is not very favorable to that as a route to happiness.

But if it’s attractive because:
- “I’ve genuinely wanted to lead people,”
- “I want to build a team and develop that capability,”

then that is **not** a purely extrinsic motive. It is closer to a self-concordant growth goal, which the literature treats much more positively.

---

## 5) Leadership roles are not automatically happier — they are mixed

The evidence here is more mixed than on interest/meaning/pay.

### Typical pattern
Leadership/management roles often bring:
- more **status**
- more **autonomy/control**
- more **influence**
- sometimes more **meaning**

But also:
- more **stress**
- more **interruptions**
- more **people problems**
- less time on the technical work that made the person successful
- more role conflict

So leadership improves satisfaction **when the person actually wants the work of management**, not just the title or the progression.

### Practical interpretation
A lot of technically strong people think they want “leadership” but actually want:
- mentorship,
- technical influence,
- strategy,
- or recognition —

not necessarily hiring, conflict management, performance reviews, alignment meetings, and staffing decisions.

Research on fit would predict:
- If you enjoy **people development, coordination, conflict handling, and team responsibility**, leadership can be deeply satisfying.
- If you mainly enjoy research and problem-solving, management can feel like a loss of the work you actually loved.

That distinction is hugely important.

---

## 6) On regret: direct evidence is weaker, but the indirect evidence is suggestive

There is less clean research specifically on:
> “Do people regret leaving interesting jobs for higher-paying less-interesting ones?”

I don’t know of a single famous study that answers that exact question cleanly.

But there are two relevant literatures.

### A) Long-term regret often centers on missed meaningful paths
Classic work by **Gilovich & Medvec** suggests that, over time, people tend to regret **inactions** more than actions — especially failing to pursue important possibilities.

That doesn’t automatically mean “take the Google role.”  
It means people often regret **not exploring a path they genuinely cared about**.

If leading a team is something you have wanted for years, then never testing that path could become its own source of regret.

### B) Self-concordance predicts better outcomes than externally driven choices
Research on **self-concordant goals** suggests people are happier when they pursue goals that feel personally authentic rather than externally rewarded.

So the regret-relevant question is not:
- “Which is objectively better?”

It’s more like:
- “Which choice would feel more like *my* choice, for reasons that actually matter to me?”

---

# What seems strongest in the literature

If I had to rank the evidence by robustness and relevance:

## Strongest findings
1. **Interesting/autonomous/meaningful work strongly predicts job satisfaction**
2. **Person-job fit matters a lot**
3. **Pay matters, but usually less than intrinsic job quality**
4. **Self-concordant goals beat externally motivated goals for well-being**

## Weaker / more conditional findings
5. **Leadership makes people happier** — only sometimes, depending on fit
6. **People regret choosing money over interest** — plausible, but the evidence is more indirect than direct

---

# If you want a research-based synthesis for your exact dilemma

The best evidence-based framing is probably this:

## You are not choosing between:
- interesting work
- vs more money

## You are choosing between:
- **higher day-to-day intellectual interest**
- vs **a less interesting domain but a role that may better fit a long-term intrinsic goal: leadership**

That means the right research question is:

> **Which job gives you the better combination of intrinsic daily experience and identity fit?**

Because the literature says both matter.

### If the Google role is less interesting in a mild way
and leadership is genuinely important to you, it could still increase long-run satisfaction.

### If it is less interesting in a severe way
and the main benefits are mostly salary/prestige, the literature would lean toward skepticism.

---

# A very concise evidence-based takeaway

If all else were equal, research would generally predict:
- **more interesting work** → higher job satisfaction and engagement
- **slightly more pay** → smaller happiness gains than people expect
- **leadership opportunity** → beneficial only if you truly like the actual work of leading

And on regret:
- People tend to regret not pursuing **personally meaningful** paths more than missing purely external rewards.
- So if leadership is a deeply meaningful aspiration, that matters a lot.
- If it is mostly symbolic, the research tilts toward staying with the more intrinsically rewarding work.

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **short reading list of the key papers/meta-analyses**, or  
2. turn this research into a **decision framework** tailored to your exact situation.

answer:
Yes — there is a fair amount of research on this, and the broad result is:

**Interesting/intrinsically rewarding work tends to matter a lot for job satisfaction and well-being, often more than modest differences in pay — especially once income is already high.**  
The evidence is much stronger for **job satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and retention** than for literal “regret” years later, but the pattern is pretty consistent.

## 1) What counts as “interesting work” in the research?
Researchers usually don’t use the word *interesting* directly. They study nearby constructs like:

- **Intrinsic motivation**: the work is enjoyable in itself
- **Autonomy**: freedom in how you do it
- **Skill variety / challenge / complexity**
- **Learning and mastery**
- **Task significance / meaning**
- **Person–job fit**: the role matches what you value and enjoy

If your current Meta role is “more interesting” because it gives you deeper technical problems, more flow, more learning, and more intellectual stimulation, that maps well onto these constructs.

---

## 2) Strong result: the nature of the work itself is a major predictor of satisfaction
A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses found that things like:

- autonomy
- variety
- feedback
- task significance
- job complexity

are **reliably associated with higher job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower withdrawal**.

A widely cited meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  This integrates a huge work-design literature and finds that motivational features of jobs are strongly related to satisfaction and motivation.

The big-picture takeaway from this literature is:

> **How the work feels day to day matters a lot.**

For knowledge workers, especially, autonomy/challenge/skill use tend to be among the strongest predictors of whether a job is satisfying.

---

## 3) Self-determination theory: intrinsic motivation usually beats extrinsic motives for well-being
Another very robust research tradition is **Self-Determination Theory** (Ryan & Deci; Gagné & Deci in work settings).

This literature says people do best when work supports:

- **Autonomy** — “I have choice/control”
- **Competence** — “I’m getting better / using my abilities”
- **Relatedness** — “I’m connected to others / having impact”

When those needs are met, people tend to show:

- more well-being
- more engagement
- better persistence
- less burnout

This is highly relevant to your case, because **“interesting research”** often strongly satisfies **autonomy + competence**, while **leading a team** can strongly satisfy **relatedness + impact + autonomy**.

So the research does **not** say “always choose the most technically interesting role.” It says:

> Choose the role that better satisfies your core intrinsic motives.

If leading people is something you’ve “always wanted,” that may not be an extrinsic perk at all — it may be a real intrinsic driver for you.

---

## 4) Pay matters, but less than most people think for job satisfaction
The best-known result here is that the link between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is real but **surprisingly small**.

A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** found the correlation between **salary level and job satisfaction** is only about **.15**.

That is a **small** relationship in practical terms.

So the evidence is roughly:

- **More pay helps**
- But it does **not** buy much job satisfaction by itself
- And a **bit higher salary** usually does less than people intuitively expect

This is especially true when you’re already in a very high-income bracket, which you almost certainly are.

---

## 5) Income affects “life evaluation” more than day-to-day emotional experience
There’s also a distinction in well-being research between:

- **Life evaluation**: “How satisfied am I with my life overall?”
- **Experienced well-being / affect**: “How do I feel day to day?”

Research from **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** suggested income keeps helping life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being rises much less after a certain point.  
Later work by **Killingsworth** and then a reconciliation paper with **Kahneman** suggests the relationship may continue at higher incomes, but the broad point still stands:

> **Extra income tends to have a smaller effect on everyday emotional experience than people expect, especially at already-high incomes.**

That matters for your choice because “interesting work” is mostly a **daily-experience variable**, while a slightly higher salary is more of a **life-evaluation / security / status variable**.

---

## 6) Meaningful work has robust links to well-being
There’s also a separate literature on **meaningful work**. Meta-analytic work by **Allan and colleagues** shows meaningful work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- more engagement
- less distress

Interesting work and meaningful work are not identical, but they often overlap.  
If your Meta research feels genuinely absorbing and valuable, that tends to be a strong positive predictor.

---

## 7) Person–job fit may be the most important concept for your exact case
For your specific dilemma, I’d pay special attention to **person–job fit** research.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

found that fit between a person and their job/organization is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction
- commitment
- intent to stay
- performance

This is relevant because you’re not comparing:

- interesting work vs boring work

You’re comparing:

- **more interesting individual-contributor/research work**
vs
- **less interesting content, but a leadership role you’ve wanted**

If leading a team fits a deep personal motive, the fit literature says that can matter a lot.

So the real research-based framing is probably:

> Which role is a better fit for your strongest long-term motives: deep research/problem-solving, or building/leading through others?

---

## 8) What about regret?
This is the part where the evidence is weaker.

There is **less direct research on “career regret”** for this exact kind of decision than there is on satisfaction and motivation. But two related findings are relevant:

### a) People often mispredict what will make them happier
Work by **Kahneman, Gilbert, Wilson**, etc. suggests people are not great at affective forecasting. They often overweight salient factors like:

- money
- prestige
- titles

and underweight:

- what daily life will actually feel like

This is sometimes called the **focusing illusion**.

### b) Daily experience tends to dominate long-run satisfaction more than people expect
Because of adaptation, one-time gains like a title bump or modest salary increase often fade in emotional impact, while **the texture of everyday work persists**.

So while I can’t cite a clean “people regret taking the less interesting job” result, the best adjacent evidence suggests:

> People often underweight day-to-day work enjoyment when making career decisions.

---

# Bottom line from the research
If I strip the literature down to the simplest evidence-based summary:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, challenging work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction and well-being.**
2. **Modest pay differences have a relatively small effect on job satisfaction**, especially at already high income.
3. **Leadership can be a major positive** if it reflects a genuine intrinsic goal, not just status.
4. **Fit** is crucial: if you truly want to lead, then the management opportunity may be more psychologically valuable than its “less interesting” content suggests.
5. Research is much stronger on **satisfaction** than on explicit **regret**, but the available evidence suggests people often underweight everyday work quality.

So, in research terms, the question is probably **not**:

- “Should I maximize interesting work or money?”

It is more:

- “Which role better satisfies my strongest intrinsic motives over the next few years?”

For many people in your income bracket, the answer will favor **interesting work**.  
For someone who has a real, long-standing desire to **lead and develop others**, the team-lead role may also be intrinsically rewarding enough to win despite being less technically interesting.

If you want, I can give you a **short reading list of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses** on this, or I can turn the evidence into a **decision matrix based on validated constructs** rather than opinions.

answer:
Yes. There is quite a lot of research relevant to this, although not much that matches your exact choice in a clean experimental way.

The short version is:

- **The nature of the work itself** — interestingness, meaning, autonomy, learning, skill use, and fit — tends to be **one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement**.
- **Pay level** matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is usually **much smaller** than the relationship of intrinsic job qualities.
- **Leadership/promotion** can improve satisfaction when it matches a real personal motive and increases autonomy/impact, but management roles also raise stress and are not automatically happier.
- On **regret**, the evidence is thinner. The closest relevant research suggests two different things:
  - people are usually happier in jobs that better satisfy intrinsic motives;
  - but people often **regret unrealized important opportunities** later, especially when the missed option was tied to a long-standing aspiration.

A few more concrete results:

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied through constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety / challenge**
- **person-job fit**

A classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses support it.

### Meta-analytic findings
A large meta-analysis of work design research found that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy, variety, significance, identity, and feedback** show **moderate to strong associations** with:
- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**

One of the best-known summaries is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Conclusion: motivational characteristics of jobs are among the strongest correlates of job satisfaction and internal work motivation.

In plain English: jobs that are more engaging and meaningful are usually experienced as better jobs, even controlling for other factors.

## 2) Meaningful work has a large association with well-being

There is now a separate literature on **meaningful work**.

A meta-analysis found that meaningful work is associated with:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- lower **turnover intentions**

A representative source:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, *Journal of Management Studies*  
  “Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis”

The key takeaway is that people generally do better when they experience their work as worthwhile and aligned with who they are.

## 3) Pay matters, but less than people often expect

There is also direct research on pay vs satisfaction.

A well-known meta-analysis found that **actual pay level** is only **weakly related** to **job satisfaction**.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
  “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis”

The rough finding often cited from that literature is:
- the correlation between **how much people are paid** and **how satisfied they are with their job** is **positive but small**.

That does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means:
- money matters a lot for **financial security**, fairness, and optionality;
- but once compensation is already high enough that basic and many comfort needs are covered, **differences in day-to-day work experience** often matter more for job satisfaction than modest pay increases.

Important nuance:
- **Pay satisfaction** is often more about **fairness/comparison** than absolute pay alone.

## 4) Intrinsic goals tend to predict better well-being than extrinsic goals

A related literature, especially from **Self-Determination Theory**, distinguishes **intrinsic** goals (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) from **extrinsic** goals (money, status, image).

Across studies, people who prioritize intrinsic goals more strongly tend to report:
- higher well-being
- greater vitality
- better functioning

Relevant work:
- **Kasser & Ryan** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations
- broader **Self-Determination Theory** research on autonomy, competence, and relatedness at work

The work version of this is: jobs that satisfy **autonomy**, **competence**, and **relatedness** tend to be experienced as much better jobs.

## 5) Person-job fit is a strong predictor

Another robust result: **fit** matters a lot.

If the role matches your interests, strengths, and values, outcomes are better.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis on person-environment fit

Higher fit is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower intentions to quit

This is relevant because “less interesting but higher status/pay” can be a classic case of worse fit despite better extrinsic rewards.

## 6) What about becoming a manager?

This is more mixed.

There is not a simple result like “people are happier as managers.”

What the research suggests is more like:
- managerial roles can increase **autonomy, impact, status, and development**
- but they also increase **role conflict, stress, interruptions, and responsibility for others**
- outcomes depend a lot on **fit** and **motivation to lead**

So if “leading a small team” is a deep aspiration for you, that is not merely an extrinsic reward. It may satisfy:
- competence
- autonomy
- impact
- identity

From a research perspective, that would place it partly on the **intrinsic** side of the ledger, not just the money/title side.

## 7) On regret: direct evidence is limited, but two literatures are relevant

This is where the evidence is less clean.

### A. Career/job well-being literature
This literature suggests people are more satisfied when work is meaningful, interesting, and fitting.

### B. Regret literature
General regret research often finds that, over longer horizons, people tend to regret **inactions** and **missed opportunities** more than actions.

The classic source is:
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** on the temporal pattern of regret

That does **not** mean “take the offer.”
It means something narrower:

- If **leading a team** is a central unrealized aspiration, turning it down might be the kind of thing that later generates regret.
- But that is a different question from **daily happiness at work**, where the literature more strongly favors interesting/meaningful work.

So the literatures point in slightly different directions:
- **day-to-day well-being:** usually favors interesting, meaningful, high-fit work;
- **possible long-run regret:** can favor acting on a major aspiration you’ve long cared about.

## 8) What the research would predict in a case like yours

Without giving a personal opinion, the literature would generally predict:

1. If the Google role is **truly much less interesting**, that would likely reduce:
   - day-to-day enjoyment
   - engagement
   - job satisfaction

2. If the salary increase is only **a bit higher**, the extra pay by itself is unlikely to compensate fully in happiness terms, especially if you are already well compensated.

3. If leading a team is something you’ve “always wanted,” that matters a lot — because leadership may itself be an **intrinsic source of meaning and growth**, not just a title bump.

4. The decision is therefore probably **not** best framed as:
   - “interesting work” vs “money”

   but more as:
   - “more interesting individual-contributor/research work”
   vs
   - “less interesting domain, but potentially more growth/agency/leadership identity”

That distinction is actually consistent with the research.

## 9) Best-supported bottom line

If I compress the evidence as much as possible:

- **Yes:** people in more interesting, meaningful, autonomous, better-fit jobs are typically **more satisfied and engaged**.
- **Yes:** those effects are generally **stronger than the effects of pay level alone**.
- **No:** there is not strong direct evidence that people who choose the more interesting job later systematically regret it.
- **But:** general regret research suggests that **foregoing a deeply valued aspiration** can become a long-term regret, even if the forgone option was not the more enjoyable day-to-day job.

## References you may want to look up

- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature. *Journal of Applied Psychology*.
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).** Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis. *Journal of Management Studies*.
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit. *Personnel Psychology*.
- **Kasser, T., & Ryan, R. M.** papers on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations.
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H. (1995).** The experience of regret: What, when, and why.

If you want, I can give you a **more rigorous research-only summary with actual effect sizes** in a small table:  
**interestingness / meaningfulness / autonomy / pay / promotion / regret**.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is pretty consistent on one big point:

**After pay is “good enough” and perceived as fair, the strongest predictors of job satisfaction tend to be intrinsic features of the work** — things like:

- how interesting/challenging it is
- autonomy
- learning/growth
- sense of meaning/impact
- fit with your strengths and values

**Raw pay level matters, but usually less than people expect.**

## What the research says

### 1) Interesting work is strongly linked to job satisfaction
In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied under constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **job characteristics**: variety, challenge, autonomy, feedback, significance
- **meaningful work**
- **person-job fit**

A classic framework is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**. A large later meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that work design variables like **autonomy, task variety, significance, and feedback** are robustly associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher internal motivation
- higher engagement
- lower turnover intentions
- better well-being

So in broad terms: **people do tend to be happier in jobs they find more interesting and psychologically rich.**

### 2) Meaningful work has a large association with well-being
More recent meta-analytic work on **meaningful work** also finds strong positive links with:

- job satisfaction
- life meaning
- general well-being

and negative links with:

- burnout
- distress
- quitting intentions

A good reference here is **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, which reviews the meaningful-work literature.

So if your current role feels genuinely more intellectually engaging or meaningful, that is not a trivial preference — it is one of the most studied predictors of satisfaction at work.

### 3) Pay matters, but less than most people think
A very relevant meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** on **pay level and job satisfaction**.

Their main result: the relationship between **actual pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly weak** — much weaker than many people intuitively expect. The famous summary statistic is roughly:

- **correlation ≈ 0.15**

That is not zero, but it is not large.

Important nuance:
- **Pay fairness / pay satisfaction** matters more than raw salary.
- Relative comparisons matter a lot.
- Once compensation is already high, marginal increases often buy less additional well-being than people expect.

For someone already in a highly paid role at Meta/Google level, the literature would generally predict that **a “bit higher salary” is unlikely by itself to dominate day-to-day work quality**.

### 4) Fit often matters more than any single factor
The literature on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** is also very relevant.

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that better fit is strongly associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower intention to quit

This matters for your case because **“leading a small team”** may or may not improve fit:

- If leading people is something you deeply want, it could increase fit a lot.
- If what you really love is hands-on research, and management would pull you away from it, it could reduce fit.

In other words, **leadership is not universally better**. It helps when it matches your motives.

### 5) Self-determination research favors autonomy, competence, and relatedness over extrinsic rewards
A huge literature from **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan and many others) shows that well-being is consistently higher when work supports three psychological needs:

- **autonomy**: I have choice/control
- **competence**: I’m growing and effective
- **relatedness**: I feel connected/respected

This literature generally finds that **intrinsic motives** and need satisfaction predict sustainable well-being better than extrinsic rewards alone.

That does not mean money is irrelevant. It means that **money without autonomy/interest/fit is often not enough**.

## What about regret specifically?

Here the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a clean literature that directly asks:  
**“Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying/managerial one?”**

What we do have are adjacent findings:

### 1) People often mispredict what will matter
Research on **affective forecasting** suggests people often overweight salient, easy-to-compare features like:

- salary
- title
- prestige

and underweight the **daily lived experience**:

- what the work actually feels like
- how much of the day is enjoyable
- whether the tasks are energizing or draining

So the literature would caution against deciding based only on headline features like compensation or “manager” status.

### 2) People adapt to many external gains
Research on **hedonic adaptation** suggests that the happiness boost from external improvements such as income or status often fades. The day-to-day nature of the work can matter more over time because you keep encountering it every day.

This doesn’t prove that interesting jobs always win. But it does support the idea that **ongoing work experience often matters more than one-time compensation differences**.

## The most research-based way to think about your specific choice

Your decision is not really:

- **interesting work vs money**

It is closer to:

- **more intrinsically interesting research role**
vs
- **less interesting domain, but more leadership/impact/autonomy/status/pay**

And research suggests the best predictor is likely:
## which option better satisfies your core motives

That means separating two questions:

answer:
Yes — there is a fairly robust literature here, though it usually studies **“interesting work” indirectly** via constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation / enjoyment of the work itself**
- **meaningful work**
- **job design**: autonomy, variety, task significance, feedback
- **person-job fit**: whether the role matches your interests/values

The short version of the research is:

> **For sustained job satisfaction and wellbeing, intrinsic features of the work usually matter more than modest differences in pay, especially once income is already high enough that basic financial stress is low.**  
> Leadership opportunities can matter a lot too — but mainly when they genuinely fit what the person wants, not just because they are “upward.”

## 1) Interesting/meaningful work is strongly linked to happiness at work

A large body of organizational psychology shows that people are more satisfied and engaged when their work has:

- **autonomy**
- **variety / skill use**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback**
- **fit with their interests**

Classic source: **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, with meta-analytic support from **Fried & Ferris (1987)** and later, broader work-design meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**.

What those meta-analyses generally show:
- **Autonomy** is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.
- **Task significance / meaningfulness** and **skill variety** also predict job satisfaction and motivation.
- Better work design is also linked to **lower burnout** and **lower turnover intentions**.

So if by “interesting” you mean “I’m genuinely engaged by the substance of the work,” the evidence is pretty favorable: people in those jobs are usually happier.

## 2) Meaningful work has a bigger relationship with wellbeing than pay does

More recent work on **meaningful work** finds fairly strong associations with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- **lower burnout**
- **lower intention to quit**

A good reference here is meta-analytic work by **Allan and colleagues** on meaningful work. The exact effect sizes vary by study, but the consistent pattern is that meaningful work has a **moderate to strong** relationship with positive work outcomes.

That doesn’t mean “meaning” causes all of that by itself — much of the evidence is correlational — but the pattern is strong and replicated.

## 3) Salary matters, but much less than people usually expect

The most relevant result here is that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**.

A standard reference is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**, a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction. The takeaway is roughly:

- higher pay does increase satisfaction,
- but the effect is **much smaller** than many people intuit,
- and it is weaker than the effects of many intrinsic job features.

That fits broader wellbeing research:
- income helps a lot when it reduces financial insecurity,
- but once someone is already well compensated, **marginal gains in wellbeing from additional pay are smaller**,
- and people show **hedonic adaptation** to income/status improvements.

On the income-happiness side, the literature is a bit debated:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found a plateau for emotional wellbeing around a certain income level.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** argued wellbeing keeps rising on average with income, but not explosively, and with diminishing practical importance at high incomes.

For someone choosing between two already-elite jobs, the literature generally suggests:
> **the day-to-day quality of the work is often a better predictor of ongoing wellbeing than a somewhat higher salary.**

## 4) People who prioritize intrinsic over extrinsic goals tend to do better psychologically

This comes from **Self-Determination Theory** and adjacent research.

Two highly relevant ideas:

### A. Need satisfaction matters
Work tends to be better for wellbeing when it supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

References: **Deci & Ryan**, **Gagné & Deci (2005)**.

### B. Intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations
Research by **Kasser & Ryan** found that people oriented more toward **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, contribution, personal development) tend to have better wellbeing than people oriented more toward **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image), even when the extrinsic goals are achieved.

That is not exactly the same as choosing between Meta and Google, but it’s highly relevant to your tradeoff:
- “more interesting work” is closer to an intrinsic good,
- “higher salary” is an extrinsic good,
- “leading a team” could be either, depending on whether it reflects a genuine aspiration or just status.

## 5) Fit matters a lot: the right job for *you* beats the “objectively better” job

The literature on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** is also very relevant.

Meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)** found that fit is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction,
- stronger commitment,
- lower turnover intentions.

This matters because your case is not simply:
- interesting work vs money.

It’s more like:
- interesting technical/research content at Meta
vs
- less interesting content, but a genuine chance to lead — which you say you have always wanted.

From a research perspective, **wanting to lead is not a trivial side note**. If leading a team is genuinely important to your sense of growth and competence, then the Google offer is not purely “extrinsic.” It may satisfy intrinsic needs too.

So the evidence does **not** say “always choose the more intellectually interesting work.”  
It says:
> choose the role with the better combination of **intrinsic motivation, autonomy, meaning, and fit**; salary differences usually matter less.

## 6) What about regret?

There is much less direct research on **career-choice regret** for this exact tradeoff.

But two adjacent findings are relevant:

### A. People adapt more to pay/status than they expect
This comes from wellbeing/adaptation research. The pleasure from higher salary or prestige often fades into the baseline more quickly than expected.

### B. People often mispredict what will make them happy
Affective forecasting research (e.g. **Gilbert & Wilson**) shows people often overweight salient, easy-to-compare features like:
- salary,
- title,
- prestige,

and underweight the features that dominate day-to-day experience:
- what they actually do all day,
- whether they find it engaging,
- who they work with,
- whether they feel autonomous.

So if you’re asking “which choice do people later regret less?”, the best research-based answer is probably:

> People often overestimate the lasting benefit of salary/title gains and underestimate the enduring importance of everyday work quality and fit.

That’s not the same as saying people regret taking the higher-paying role. But it does suggest the common decision bias runs in that direction.

## 7) The most research-grounded takeaway for your specific situation

If I translate the literature into your case, the important variables are probably:

1. **How much does the work itself interest you?**
2. **How much autonomy/control would each role give you?**
3. **How strong is your real desire to lead people?**
4. **Will the team-leading role give you growth and meaning, or just admin and meetings?**
5. **How large is the salary difference in practical terms, given your current income?**

The literature would predict roughly:

- If the salary bump is only modest, and both jobs already give you high financial security, **the extra money alone is unlikely to produce a large or lasting wellbeing gain**.
- If the Meta role is much more engaging/meaningful on a daily basis, that is a **serious point in its favor**.
- But if leading a small team is a deep aspiration and the Google role would satisfy autonomy/competence/growth in a way Meta currently does not, then that leadership opportunity could matter **as much as or more than “interestingness” of the technical content**.

So the research answer is not:
- “always stay in the more interesting job,”

but rather:
- **interesting, meaningful, well-designed work usually predicts happiness better than a modest pay increase;**
- **however, a well-fitting leadership opportunity can also be a strong intrinsic source of wellbeing.**

## If you want the most relevant papers to read

A compact reading list:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on **work design** (autonomy, variety, significance, etc.)
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on **person-environment fit**
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — Self-Determination Theory in work settings
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and wellbeing
- **Allan et al.** — meta-analytic work on **meaningful work**
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and wellbeing
- **Gilbert & Wilson** — affective forecasting / misprediction of future happiness

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page research summary specifically for your decision**, with:
- the strongest empirical findings,
- likely effect sizes in plain English,
- and a decision framework grounded in the literature rather than opinion.

answer:
Yes. The literature is not perfect for a choice exactly like “Meta research IC role vs Google team lead role,” but there are some very consistent findings.

## Short version

Across organizational psychology, **interesting/intrinsically motivating work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement**.  
**Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already high/fair.**  
And **fit** matters a lot: people tend to do better when the role matches what they actually enjoy and value.

So the research does **not** say “always choose the more interesting job.” But it does say that, especially for already well-paid professionals, **interestingness / intrinsic motivation / autonomy / meaning are not minor luxuries — they are major drivers of satisfaction**.

A key nuance in your case: **leading a team may itself be intrinsically meaningful** if that is something you genuinely want. So your real tradeoff may not be “interesting vs money”; it may be **technical/research interest vs leadership interest**.

---

## What the research says

### 1) Interesting work strongly predicts satisfaction and motivation

A large body of work on **job design** finds that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- a sense of **meaningfulness**

This goes back to **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, and later meta-analyses found these characteristics have **moderate to strong associations** with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions.

Representative result:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** meta-analysis: job characteristics like autonomy, variety, and meaningfulness-related features were strongly related to **job satisfaction**, **motivation**, and **reduced withdrawal**.

In plain English: people are usually happier when the work itself is engaging, varied, meaningful, and gives them room to operate.

---

### 2) Intrinsic motivation is strongly linked to well-being

**Self-Determination Theory** is one of the most established frameworks here. It says people thrive when work supports:

- **autonomy** — “I have choice/control”
- **competence** — “I’m growing and effective”
- **relatedness** — “I feel connected/respected”

When jobs satisfy those needs, people tend to show more:

- well-being
- vitality
- engagement
- persistence

and less:

- burnout
- cynicism
- ill-being

This is relevant because “interesting work” often supports **autonomy + competence** very strongly.

Representative sources:
- **Deci & Ryan (2000)**
- later workplace reviews/meta-analytic syntheses on need satisfaction at work

---

### 3) Pay matters, but less than most people think

There is a robust finding that **pay and happiness/satisfaction are positively related**, but the relationship is usually **smaller than for intrinsic work features**.

In job-attitudes research:
- **pay level** tends to have only a **small-to-moderate** relationship with **job satisfaction**
- **pay fairness** and **relative pay** often matter more than absolute pay
- once people are already well-compensated, additional income often has **diminishing psychological returns**

A widely cited meta-analysis:
- **Judge et al. (2010)** found the relationship between **pay level and job satisfaction** is real but **modest**, much weaker than many people assume.

So if the Google offer is only “a bit higher salary,” the literature would generally treat that as **meaningful but not dominant**, especially at already high income levels.

---

### 4) Fit with your interests predicts satisfaction

Another strong line of research is **person-job fit** and **interest congruence**.

People tend to report better outcomes when:
- their job matches their interests
- the work fits their strengths/identity
- the organizational environment fits their values

These outcomes include:
- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower intention to quit
- sometimes better performance

Representative source:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis on person-environment fit

So if your current role is genuinely much more intellectually compelling to you, that is not trivial; it is a variable the literature says matters.

---

### 5) Meaningful work is a big predictor of well-being

A related literature on **meaningful work** shows that when people feel their work is worthwhile, aligned with values, and important, they tend to report substantially better:

- job satisfaction
- general well-being
- engagement
- commitment

Meaning is not exactly the same thing as “interesting,” but in practice they often overlap for researchers and engineers.

So if your Meta role feels genuinely exciting and meaningful, that would generally count as a major positive in the research.

---

### 6) Promotions and leadership are not automatically better

This part is important for your case.

A lot of people assume:
> more responsibility + more pay + leadership title = more satisfaction

The evidence is more mixed.

Leadership roles can improve satisfaction when they bring:
- more autonomy
- more influence
- more status
- more meaning
- stronger growth

But they can reduce satisfaction when they mean:
- less time on the work you actually enjoy
- more meetings/coordination/admin
- more conflict management
- role overload
- responsibility without true control

So the best-supported conclusion is:

> **Leadership helps when it fits what you actually want and are motivated by.**

Since you say “leading a small team is something I always wanted,” this matters a lot. That desire means the leadership aspect is not merely external prestige; it may be a genuine intrinsic motivator for you.

In research terms, that changes the comparison.

---

## What about regret?

There is some classic psychological research on regret, but it is less directly applicable than the job satisfaction literature.

A famous finding:
- **short term**: people often regret **actions**
- **long term**: people more often regret **inactions** or paths not taken

This comes from work by **Gilovich & Medvec**.

But two cautions:
1. this is **not specific to career decisions**
2. it does **not** imply “take every exciting offer”

What it does suggest, cautiously, is that people often regret **not exploring identity-relevant opportunities**.

For you, the tricky part is that there may be **two identity-relevant opportunities**:
- doing highly interesting research
- becoming a leader

So regret research alone will not resolve this.

---

## If you want the research-based bottom line

The literature points to something like this:

### People tend to be happier at work when they have:
1. **interesting / intrinsically motivating tasks**
2. **autonomy**
3. **competence/growth**
4. **meaning**
5. **fit with their interests/values**
6. **good relationships and good manager**
7. **fair pay**

If pay is already high, **moving from “very interesting” to “much less interesting” for a modest salary increase is often not a good happiness trade**, according to the broad research.

But your case has a twist:

- If **leading a team** is deeply important to you,
- and the Google role would genuinely satisfy your needs for **growth, autonomy, impact, and identity**,

then it may still be the better move, **despite the work itself being less intrinsically interesting**.

Put differently:

> Research would probably say the biggest question is not “salary bump vs interestingness.”  
> It is “which role better satisfies your deeper motives: research curiosity, or leadership growth?”

---

## What the evidence most strongly argues against

The literature most strongly argues against these common mistakes:

### Mistake 1: Overweighting salary
A modest increase in already-high compensation often has less effect on long-run satisfaction than people predict.

### Mistake 2: Treating “interesting work” as a luxury
Interesting work is strongly tied to motivation, engagement, and well-being.

### Mistake 3: Treating management as a pure upgrade
Management is a different job, not just a better version of the current one.

---

## If you want a more formal evidence-based way to compare the two

A research-grounded decision framework would score each option on:

- **intrinsic interest**
- **autonomy**
- **learning/growth**
- **meaning/impact**
- **leadership fit**
- **manager/team quality**
- **workload/stress**
- **pay/fairness**
- **future optionality**

That would be closer to what the literature actually says than simply comparing salary and title.

---

## Representative references

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.  
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000).** The “what” and “why” of goal pursuits: Human needs and self-determination.  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, etc.  
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H.** classic work on regret over actions vs inactions.

If you want, I can turn this into a **research-based decision matrix** for your exact Meta-vs-Google choice, without giving personal-opinion advice.

answer:
Yes. There isn’t one definitive study on your exact choice, but several research literatures converge on a pretty clear pattern:

**For people who are already well-paid, the nature of the work itself usually predicts ongoing job satisfaction more strongly than incremental pay.**  
And **whether a move into management increases happiness depends a lot on person–role fit**: people who genuinely want leadership often benefit; people who mainly value the technical/creative content often don’t.

Here’s the evidence base, in a research-first way.

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In the literature, “interesting work” is usually captured indirectly through things like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **task variety**
- **autonomy**
- **skill use / challenge**
- **meaningfulness**
- **learning opportunities**
- **person-job fit**

### Job design research
A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later summarized in meta-analyses.

The core result is that jobs are more satisfying when they provide:

- variety
- autonomy
- feedback
- task significance / meaningfulness
- identity/ownership of work

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007), Psychological Bulletin**  
  Reviewed a very large body of work design studies and found that **motivational job characteristics** were strongly related to **job satisfaction, internal work motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions**.

This is one of the strongest recurring findings in organizational psychology: **the content and structure of the work matter a lot**.

---

## 2) Meaningful/intrinsically motivating work is strongly associated with well-being

A related literature looks at **meaningful work**.

### Meaningful work meta-analyses
Meta-analytic reviews have found that meaningful work is positively associated with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- **organizational commitment**
- and negatively associated with **turnover intentions**

One useful summary is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** / related meta-analytic work on meaningful work  
  The general finding is that people who experience their work as meaningful report **substantially better work-related well-being**.

Important caveat: most of this evidence is correlational, but the consistency is very strong, and intervention/job redesign studies also point in the same causal direction.

### Self-Determination Theory
Another highly relevant framework is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan).

At work, well-being is higher when the job supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that are interesting, challenging, and let you exercise mastery generally score better on these dimensions.

Reviews/meta-analyses in this tradition consistently show that **need satisfaction at work predicts job satisfaction, vitality, engagement, and lower burnout**.

So if “interesting” for you means “I learn, create, solve hard problems, and feel agency,” the research says that’s not a minor preference; it is central to work well-being.

---

## 3) Salary matters, but pay level by itself is usually a weaker predictor of job satisfaction than people expect

This is one of the most robust findings.

### Pay level vs job satisfaction
A key meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
  Found that the relationship between **actual pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

In plain English:
- Higher pay does make a difference.
- But the difference is usually **much smaller** than people assume.
- Especially once compensation is already high enough that basic financial stress is not the binding constraint.

What tends to matter more than raw pay:
- whether pay feels **fair**
- whether it compares well to peers/reference group
- whether it matches your market value and contribution
- whether the total role feels worth it

So the evidence does **not** say salary is irrelevant. It says that **a somewhat higher salary is rarely a dominant predictor of long-term job satisfaction on its own**, particularly for already high earners.

---

## 4) Person-job fit matters a lot — often more than generic “better” features

This may be the most important part for your specific case.

### Person-environment fit research
A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
  Found that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** are strongly associated with:
  - job satisfaction
  - organizational commitment
  - lower intent to quit
  - better performance-related outcomes

This matters because your choice is not simply:
- interesting work vs management
but:
- **technical individual-contributor work that fits your interests**
vs
- **leadership work that may fit a long-standing aspiration**

If leading a team is something you “always wanted,” then from a fit perspective that is not a trivial add-on. Leadership may satisfy values around:
- growth
- status
- responsibility
- mentoring
- impact through others

Research suggests that when a role matches someone’s motives and identity, satisfaction is higher. When it doesn’t, even objectively attractive roles can disappoint.

So the literature would predict:

- If you are fundamentally motivated by **hands-on research and technical novelty**, the less-interesting Google role may reduce satisfaction despite more pay.
- If you are deeply motivated by **leading, mentoring, and multiplying impact through a team**, that could offset or exceed the loss of topical interest.

---

## 5) Promotions and management roles often increase status and pay, but not always happiness

The evidence here is more mixed.

There are panel studies suggesting that promotions can lead to:

- short-term increases in satisfaction/status
- but also more stress, longer hours, and more responsibility
- with some gains fading over time

A common finding in the broader promotion literature is:
- **promotions often improve career satisfaction or status evaluation**
- but they do **not reliably produce large, lasting gains in overall well-being**

Why? Likely because:
- hedonic adaptation kicks in
- workload/stress increase
- the day-to-day work changes in ways some people like and others don’t

This is especially relevant in technical fields, where moving into management often means giving up the part of the work people originally found intrinsically rewarding.

So research would caution against assuming:
> “leading a team” = “I will definitely be happier”

It often helps people who truly like management work. It often disappoints people who mainly like being recognized as senior/high-status but don’t enjoy the actual daily tasks of managing.

---

## 6) Intrinsic motives tend to predict well-being better than extrinsic motives

There is a broader well-being literature, especially from **Kasser & Ryan** and related work, showing that prioritizing **intrinsic aspirations** (growth, relationships, community, mastery) is generally associated with better well-being than prioritizing **extrinsic aspirations** (money, status, image), all else equal.

This does **not** mean money and status are bad. It means that, as primary drivers, they tend to be weaker sources of sustained well-being than intrinsic engagement and fit.

Applied to your case:
- “I’ll make more” is real, but often less durable psychologically
- “I’ll spend my days doing work I find compelling” often has more day-to-day impact
- unless the management aspect itself is intrinsically compelling to you

---

## 7) Regret: there’s less direct evidence, but the closest research suggests people regret misfit more than forgone money

Direct “career regret” evidence is less developed than job satisfaction evidence. But adjacent findings point to this:

- People are bad at **affective forecasting**: they often overestimate the long-term happiness impact of pay/status gains.
- People adapt relatively quickly to **income/status changes**.
- Daily experience is driven heavily by what you actually do all day: the work, the people, the autonomy, the stress level.

So while I can’t honestly say “the literature proves people regret the higher-paying less-interesting job,” I can say:

**The better-supported pattern is that lasting work well-being depends more on day-to-day experience and fit than on incremental compensation gains.**

That is very close to your question.

---

## 8) A concise ranking of what research usually finds most predictive

For already-successful professionals, long-term work satisfaction tends to be strongly shaped by:

1. **Interesting/meaningful work**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Fit with your motives/identity**
4. **Manager and team quality**
5. **Workload / stress / work-life impact**
6. **Learning and growth**
7. **Fair compensation**
8. **Absolute pay level**

That ordering is a simplification, but it’s roughly the direction of the evidence.

---

## 9) What the evidence would predict in a case like yours

Not advice — just an evidence-based interpretation.

### If the Google role is:
- only **slightly** less interesting,
- gives real leadership responsibility,
- and you have a strong genuine desire to manage,

then research on **fit, growth, and motivation** says it could plausibly improve satisfaction despite the weaker topical interest.

### If the Google role is:
- **much** less interesting,
- management-heavy in a way that takes you away from the work you love,
- and the salary increase is only modest,

then the literature would generally predict that **the more interesting role is more likely to produce higher ongoing job satisfaction**.

### If both are already highly paid:
then the extra salary is less likely to be decisive for happiness than:
- whether you enjoy the actual daily work
- whether you want to lead people enough to accept losing some technical interest

---

## 10) Best research-backed takeaway in one sentence

**A modest increase in salary usually does less for long-term happiness than people expect, while interesting/meaningful work and role fit are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction — but leadership can improve well-being if, and mainly if, management itself fits your motives.**

---

## References / literatures to look up

If you want to read the actual research, I’d start with these:

- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).**  
  *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
  **Psychological Bulletin**

- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).**  
  *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
  **Journal of Vocational Behavior**

- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).**  
  *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
  **Personnel Psychology**

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976/1980)**  
  Work design / Job Characteristics Model

- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M.**  
  Self-Determination Theory; workplace need satisfaction research

- **Allan, B. A.** and colleagues on **meaningful work** meta-analytic research

- **Kasser, T., & Ryan, R. M.**  
  Intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being

- On income and well-being more broadly:  
  **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**, **Killingsworth (2021)**, and later reconciliation work

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **short annotated bibliography** with the strongest papers most relevant to your exact dilemma, or  
2. turn this into a **decision framework based only on research variables** (interest, pay delta, leadership fit, stress, future option value, etc.).
